did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for dermatology tasks. Specifically, it employs the U-Net architecture for semantic segmentation of dermoscopic skin lesions on the PH2 dataset and a pretrained MobileNet architecture for classifying skin lesions into 7 categories using the HAM-10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper explicitly labels itself as a 'Research paper' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the implementation of U-Net for segmentation on the PH2 dataset and MobileNet for classification on the HAM-10000 dataset, reporting specific validation accuracies (95% and 84.73% respectively) and loss metrics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Segmentation and Classification of Dermatoscopic Skin Lesion images using U-Net and MobileNet models
journal: IJFANS INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF FOOD AND NUTRITIONAL SCIENCES
brief_summary: This study proposes a methodology to improve skin lesion classification by integrating semantic segmentation as a preprocessing step. The U-Net architecture was employed for segmentation on the PH2 dataset, achieving a validation accuracy of 95%. Subsequently, a pretrained MobileNet architecture was used to classify lesions into seven categories using the HAM-10000 dataset. The researchers compared classification performance across three datasets: unmasked images, masked images, and cropped images derived from segmentation masks. The MobileNet model achieved the highest validation accuracy of 84.73% on the cropped dataset, outperforming the unmasked (82%) and masked (79%) variants. The results indicate that segmenting the lesion area prior to classification enhances the performance of the lightweight CNN classifier.

all_task_types.target: Semantic segmentation mask of the lesion area
all_task_types.details: The U-Net architecture was employed for semantic segmentation of dermoscopic skin lesions on the PH2 dataset. The model predicted binary masks to extract the Region of Interest (ROI). Performance metrics included IoU (96.06%), Dice Coefficient (91.97%), Precision (92.69%), Recall (93.84%), and Accuracy (95.69%).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: A pretrained MobileNet architecture was used to classify skin lesions into 7 distinct classes (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanocytic nevus, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion, and one other implied by the 7 classes) using the HAM-10000 dataset. The model used a dense layer with 7 neurons and softmax activation. The primary task evaluated classification performance on three input variations: unmasked images (82% accuracy), masked images (79% accuracy), and cropped images derived from segmentation masks (84.73% accuracy).

datasets_used.name: HAM-10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-sourced dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, containing 10,015 labelled images of size 450x600.

use.training: Used as the source for three derived datasets (unmasked, masked, and cropped) to train the MobileNet classification model.
use.validation: Used to evaluate classification performance (validation accuracy reported as 84.73% for cropped dataset).
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification performance (test accuracy reported as 84% for cropped dataset).
use.other: Ground truth masks from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset were used to create the masked and cropped variants.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database obtained using the Tuebinger Mole Analyzer equipment at the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano in Matosinhos, Portugal. Images are 768x560 pixels in 8-bit RGB color format with 20x magnification.

use.training: Used to train and fine-tune the U-Net architecture for semantic segmentation.
use.validation: Used to evaluate segmentation performance (validation accuracy of 95% reported).
use.testing: Used to evaluate segmentation performance (test accuracy of 95.69% reported).
use.other: Used to extract lesion segmentation masks for the testing dataset of the classification task.

datasets_used.name: Dataset1 (Unmasked HAM-10000)
datasets_used.description: The original unmasked images from the HAM-10000 dataset.

use.training: Used to train the MobileNet classification model.
use.validation: Used to evaluate classification performance (validation accuracy of 82% reported).
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification performance (test accuracy of 82% reported).

datasets_used.name: Dataset2 (Masked HAM-10000)
datasets_used.description: The original images of the HAM-10000 dataset overlaid with binary masks from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset.

use.training: Used to train the MobileNet classification model.
use.validation: Used to evaluate classification performance (validation accuracy of 79% reported).
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification performance (test accuracy of 79% reported).

datasets_used.name: Dataset3 (Cropped HAM-10000)
datasets_used.description: Images from the HAM-10000 dataset where the contour parts of binary masks were extracted using CV2 and the original image was cropped along the contour part.

use.training: Used to train the MobileNet classification model.
use.validation: Used to evaluate classification performance (validation accuracy of 84.73% reported).
use.testing: Used to evaluate classification performance (test accuracy of 84% reported).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between datasets were made using accuracy and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

PH2.notes: The U-Net architecture was trained on the PH2 dataset. Table-3 compares the proposed U-Net against a Modified U-Net (ResNet in decoder), showing the proposed method achieved 0.95 accuracy vs 0.89 for the modified version.

Dataset2 (Masked).notes: Original images overlaid with binary masks from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset.

Dataset3 (Cropped).notes: Images cropped along the contour part of binary masks. This dataset performed best among the three.

comparison.notes: Table-4 compares the proposed method against VGG-16. The proposed method achieved higher accuracy and F1 score.

architecture_used.pretraining: MobileNet was initialized with pretrained weights from the ImageNet dataset. U-Net was fine-tuned on the PH2 dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: MobileNet requires 224x224x3 RGB images. PH2 dataset images are 768x560 pixels. HAM-10000 dataset images are 450x600 pixels.

architecture_details.classification_model: MobileNet architecture (lightweight CNN). A dense layer with 7 neurons was added for the 7-class classification task. Activation functions used: ReLU and Softmax.

training_details.optimizer: Adam optimizer with a starting learning rate of 0.001, later fine-tuned using callbacks.
training_details.augmentation: ImageDataGenerator used with rotation_range=180, width_shift_range=0.1, height_shift_range=0.1, zoom_range=0.1, horizontal_flip=True, vertical_flip=True, brightness_range=[0.9, 1.1], fill_mode='nearest'.

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Binary mask of the lesion area.
classification_or_output_head.classification_output: 7-class probability distribution via dense layer with 7 neurons and softmax activation.

preprocessing_techniques: Semantic segmentation using U-Net architecture to extract lesion Region of Interest (ROI) masks from the PH2 dataset.
Application of ground truth binary masks from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset to create masked images (Dataset2).
Cropping of original images along the contour parts of binary masks using OpenCV (CV2) to create cropped images (Dataset3).

Image augmentation using ImageDataGenerator with the following parameters: rotation_range=180, width_shift_range=0.1, height_shift_range=0.1, zoom_range=0.1, horizontal_flip=True, vertical_flip=True, brightness_range=[0.9, 1.1], fill_mode='nearest'.
Resizing of input images to 224x224x3 RGB format for the MobileNet architecture.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to all three datasets (unmasked, masked, and cropped) to increase dataset heterogeneity and alleviate data insufficiency. Specific parameters: rotation_range=180, width_shift_range=0.1, height_shift_range=0.1, zoom_range=0.1, horizontal_flip=True, vertical_flip=True, brightness_range=[0.9, 1.1], fill_mode='nearest'.

conclusions: Integrating semantic segmentation as a preprocessing step improves the performance of CNN-based skin lesion classification models.

The U-Net architecture achieved a test accuracy of 95.69% for semantic segmentation of dermoscopic skin lesions on the PH2 dataset, outperforming a modified U-Net with a ResNet decoder (89% accuracy).

The MobileNet architecture, when trained on cropped images derived from segmentation masks, achieved the highest classification accuracy (84.73% validation, 84% test) compared to unmasked (82%) and masked (79%) datasets.
The proposed MobileNet method on cropped datasets outperformed the VGG-16 model, achieving a test accuracy of 84.53% and an F1 score of 0.74 compared to VGG-16's 79.71% accuracy and 0.597 F1 score.

The performance of the classification model degrades when evaluating images segmented by the proposed U-Net model, likely because the training masks were sourced from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset rather than generated by the proposed segmentation model.
Future work should focus on training the classification model using masks generated by the proposed segmentation model to address the identified limitation.
important_remarks: The classification model was trained using ground truth masks from the HAM-10000 lesion segmentation dataset rather than masks generated by the proposed U-Net segmentation model, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation that may degrade performance if the segmentation model is used for inference.

The study lacks external validation; the segmentation model was trained and tested on the PH2 dataset, while the classification model was trained and tested on the HAM-10000 dataset, with no cross-dataset evaluation reported.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements between the unmasked, masked, and cropped datasets.

The segmentation performance on the PH2 dataset (95.69% accuracy) is significantly lower than state-of-the-art benchmarks typically reported for this dataset, which may impact the reliability of the ROI extraction for classification.

The paper does not report the specific class-wise distribution or balance of the HAM-10000 dataset used for training, which is critical for evaluating potential bias in the 7-class classification task.
The journal (IJFANS) is listed as UGC CARE Group-I but is not a high-impact medical or computer vision journal, which may affect the perceived rigor of the peer review process.
